rationale: The page contains two significant comparative and historical tables: one comparing the legal status and benefits of NRIs, PIOs, and OCI cardholders, and another detailing the early evolution of the UPSC starting with the Cornwallis Code of 1793. This is high-yield content for polity and governance sections of competitive exams.
cleanedText:
9.2. APPENDIX 2: NRI VS PIO VS OCI
Parameters: Who
- Non-Resident Indian (NRI): Indian citizen who ordinarily residing outside India and holds an Indian Passport.
- Person of Indian Origin (PIO): Person who or whose any of ancestors was an Indian national and who is presently holding another country’s citizenship/ nationality i.e. he/she holding foreign passport.
- OCI Cardholders: Person registered as OCI Cardholder under section 7A of Citizenship Act, 1955.
Parameters: What benefits one is entitled to?
- Non-Resident Indian (NRI): All benefits as available to Indian citizens subject to notifications issued by Government from time to time.
- Person of Indian Origin (PIO): No specific benefits.
- OCI Cardholders:
* Multiple entry lifelong visa.
* Exemption from registration for any length of stay in India.
* Parity with NRIs in economic, financial, and educational fields except acquisition of agricultural properties.
* Treated at par with NRIs in inter-country adoption of Indian children, domestic air fares, and eligible for National Pension Scheme.
* Charged domestic entry fees for visiting national parks and wildlife sanctuaries.
Parameters: Does he/she require visa for visiting India?
- Non-Resident Indian (NRI): No.
- Person of Indian Origin (PIO): Yes.
- OCI Cardholders: Can visit India without visa for life long.
Parameters: Is he/she required to register with local police authorities in India?
- Non-Resident Indian (NRI): No.
- Person of Indian Origin (PIO): Yes, if period of stay is for more than 180 days.
- OCI Cardholders: No.
Parameters: What activities can be undertaken in India?
- Non-Resident Indian (NRI): All activities.
- Person of Indian Origin (PIO): Activity as per the type of visa obtained.
- OCI Cardholders: All activities except research work for which special permission is required from Indian Mission/Post/ FRRO concerned.
Parameters: How can one acquire Indian citizenship?
- Non-Resident Indian (NRI): He/she is an Indian citizen.
- Person of Indian Origin (PIO): As per section 5(1)(a) & 5(1)(c) of Citizenship Act, 1955, he/she has to be ordinarily resident in India for 7 years before apply for registration.
- OCI Cardholders: As per section 5(1)(g) of Citizenship Act 1955, OCI cardholder registered for five years and ordinarily resident in India for twelve months before apply for registration.
9.3. APPENDIX 3: EVOLUTION OF UPSC
Development: Cornwallis Code, 1793
Outcome: Governor-General Lord Cornwallis (father of civil services in India) laid the foundation of Indian Civil Services (ICS).
